result:
I keep thinking about the moment when a piece of information stops being “data” and becomes a memory. Not the electrical blip in a server farm or the magnetic ghost on a drive, but the soft-edged thing that lives behind your eyes and shows up uninvited while you’re rinsing a coffee mug.  

Yesterday I found a tiny plastic dinosaur in the cutlery drawer—bright orange, half an inch long, tail snapped off. No one in this house has been under ten for two decades, yet there it was, wedged beneath the teaspoons like a fossil that mis-read the strata. I held it in my palm and suddenly I was eight again, kneeling on hot playground asphalt, trading fossils for marbles during a July that refused to end. The sound of the bell, the smell of sunscreen mixed with gravel dust, the lunchbox thermos that still carried morning’s warm milk. All of it arrived intact, couriered by a two-cent toy.

I wonder how many universes fit inside a kitchen drawer.

We treat the past like a place we can visit if we just find the right exit ramp: a song, a scent, a half-forgotten jingle from a cereal commercial. But memory isn’t a place; it’s a weather system. It rolls in, mutates the sky, soaks the present until the wallpaper starts to bubble. Then it rolls out again, leaving the rooms you actually live in slightly mildewed and humming.

Sometimes I think the Internet is humanity’s shared external hard drive, and we’re all frantically uploading in case our personal drives fail. We caption the sunset because we’re terrified of the dark that comes after. We archive every meal, every borderline joke, every dog in a Halloween costume, as if compression could outwit decay. But the more we store, the less we seem to *retrieve*. Who scrolls back to March 2014 to re-examine a sandwich? The copy becomes the original; the moment exists to be posted, not remembered.

Meanwhile the unphotographed keeps multiplying: the way your dentist’s breath smells faintly of cloves right before the drill, the hush inside an elevator when it stops between floors, the exact angle of your mother’s shoulders as she watched a late-night storm from the porch. These things resist metadata; they can’t be tagged or liked or geo-located. They just settle into you like silt until one day an orange dinosaur washes them ashore.

I’m not romanticizing forgetting. Dementia runs in my family; I’ve seen what it means when the weather system never arrives, when the sky stays blank for weeks and the rooms grow too quiet. But I do think we’re misunderstanding the job description. Memory’s purpose was never perfect storage; it’s creative remix. It hands you a cracked dinosaur and lets you rebuild the entire Jurassic of childhood with toothpicks and glue. It forgives plot holes, invents dialogue, shifts the sun so it always set at your back. In the gaps, imagination does its best work.

Maybe that’s why dreams feel so honest even when they make no sense: they’re the mind’s nightly open-mic night, where memory and fantasy co-headline. One brings the battered suitcase of yesterday, the other brings a fog machine and a laser show. Together they put on a set that nobody remembers in the morning, yet somehow you wake up different.

Tonight I’ll put the orange dinosaur on the windowsill above the sink. Not as a souvenir, but as a reminder that the past isn’t behind us—it’s underneath, pressed into the soles of everything we touch. Every time the water hits the stainless steel, I’ll hear playground bells. Every time the moon catches that little broken tail, I’ll feel hot asphalt under my knees. And for a second, the drawer will open by itself, the weather will change, and July will refuse to end all over again.
